title: Data Validation with pydantic
subtitle: Bean Validation + Jackson, driven by type hints

sections.java: // POJO + Jackson + Bean Validation public class User { @NotNull private String name; @Email private String email; @Min(0) private int age; // + getters, setters, constructor... } // ObjectMapper + Validator to parse & check
sections.python: # One model: parsing + validation + types from pydantic import BaseModel, EmailStr, Field class User(BaseModel): name: str email: EmailStr age: int = Field(ge=0) # Validate straight from a dict / JSON: user = User.model_validate(payload)

sections.title: Your Type Hints Become the Schema
sections.content: pydantic reads the same annotations you'd write anyway and turns them into runtime parsing + validation + coercion. User.model_validate(json_dict) raises a precise ValidationError listing every bad field — the Jackson + Hibernate Validator combo, in one class with no getters/setters. user.model_dump() serializes back to a dict; model_dump_json() to JSON.

sections.title: pydantic Validates at Runtime — Plain Hints Don't
sections.content: Module 21 stressed that type hints are not enforced at runtime. pydantic is the exception that proves the rule: it actively checks values against the annotations when you construct or validate a model. So User(age="oops") raises, while a normal class with age: int would happily store the string. Use pydantic at your system's boundaries (API input, config, message payloads); use plain dataclasses for trusted internal data.

sections.java: // Nested objects + custom validation public class Order { @Valid List<Item> items; @AssertTrue boolean hasItems() { return !items.isEmpty(); } }
sections.python: # Nested models + a field validator from pydantic import field_validator class Order(BaseModel): items: list[Item] # nested, auto-validated @field_validator("items") @classmethod def not_empty(cls, v): if not v: raise ValueError("empty order") return v

sections.question: How does pydantic differ from a plain class with type hints?
options: It doesn't — both ignore the annotations at runtime
pydantic enforces the annotations at runtime, parsing and validating values (and raising ValidationError)
pydantic removes the need for type hints
sections.explanation: A normal class treats hints as inert documentation. A pydantic BaseModel uses them as a live schema — it coerces and validates input when you create or model_validate an instance, raising a detailed ValidationError on bad data. That makes it ideal for untrusted boundaries (API bodies, config, queue messages).
